subject: PATIENT
heading: TEST RESULTS :: WHAT DOES MY BIOPSY MEAN?__page_no__2
text: test results what does my biopsy mean? helicobacter pylorus is a bacteria that can live in the acid of the stomach and can lead to inflamation and increases the risk of developing ulcers. this bacteria has been linked to an increased risk of stomach cancer. for all of the above reasons we will often recommend treatment for this bacteria. it is crucial to take every dose of medicine prescribed as the bacteria is hard to kill if doses of medicine are missed. h. pylori is a type of bacteria that infects your stomach. it can damage the tissue in your stomach and the first part of your small intestine (the duodenum). this can cause redness and soreness (inflammation). in some cases it can also cause painful sores called peptic ulcers in your upper digestive tract. the treatment is a 2 week course of antibiotics. after completing the antibiotics, you will need to be off acid medication for another 2 weeks and then go to the lab for a urea breath test or stool antigen test which is a test to confirm that you have cleared the infection. please feel free to contact the office if you have any further questions. a copy of the test result(s) were sent to [provider_name].

subject: PATIENT
heading: Medications: :: Procedure:__page_no__4
text: medications: procedure: prior to the procedure, a history and physical was performed, and patient medications and allergies were reviewed. the patient's tolerance of previous anesthesia was also reviewed. the risks and benefits of the procedure and the sedation options and risks were discussed with the patient. all questions were answered, and informed consent was obtained. prior anticoagulants: the patient has taken no anticoagulant or antiplatelet agents. asa grade assessment: ii - a patient with mild systemic disease. after reviewing the risks and benefits, the patient was deemed in satisfactory condition to undergo the procedure. prior aspirin/ nsaid therapy: the patient has taken no aspirin or nsaid medications. the scope # 665 was introduced through the anus and advanced to the cecum, identified by appendiceal orifice and ileocecal valve. the colonoscopy was performed without difficulty. the patient tolerated the procedure well. the quality of the bowel preparation was adequate. the ileocecal valve, appendiceal orifice, and rectum were photographed.

subject: PATIENT
heading: CHIEF COMPLAINT/HPI:__page_no__10
text: chief complaint/hpi: the patient presents for expedited hospital follow-up after an episode of slow transit constipation. as you know, she is an 81-year-old female with history of moderate copd. she presented to the ed on [encounter_date] with generalized weakness and upper abdominal pain. ultrasound notes stable liver hemangioma and normal gallbladder. noncontrast ap ct shows diverticulosis of the sigmoid colon without acute diverticulitis. labs with normal lfts, mildly elevated creatinine. troponins are normal and hemoglobin stable at 13.1. she was treated with lactulose with success. she also notes heartburn and had been prescribed famotidine though she believes this worsened symptoms. she describes epigastric discomfort, denies nausea or vomiting. she has overall lost her appetite. constipation has improved with lactulose twice daily. she did discontinue famotidine as she believes this worsened symptoms. she last had colonoscopy in 2014 notable for a hyperplastic polyp and diverticulosis only. denies history of endoscopy. she did have bowel resection due to recurrent diverticulitis in 2013.

text: chief complaint/hpi: the patient presents for expedited hospital follow-up after an episode of slow transit constipation. as you know, she is an 81-year-old female with history of moderate copd. she presented to the ed on [encounter_date] with generalized weakness and upper abdominal pain. ultrasound notes stable liver hemangioma and normal gallbladder. noncontrast ap ct shows diverticulosis of the sigmoid colon without acute diverticulitis. labs with normal lfts, mildly elevated creatinine. troponins are normal and hemoglobin stable at 13.1. she was treated with lactulose with success. she also notes heartburn and had been prescribed famotidine though she believes this worsened symptoms. she describes epigastric discomfort, denies nausea or vomiting. she has overall lost her appetite. constipation has improved with lactulose twice daily. she did discontinue famotidine as she believes this worsened symptoms. she last had colonoscopy in 2014 notable for a hyperplastic polyp and diverticulosis only. denies history of endoscopy. she did have bowel resection due to recurrent diverticulitis in 2013.

subject: PATIENT
heading: PLAN:__page_no__12
text: plan: in summary, this is an 81-year-old female presenting for expedited hospital follow-up after an episode of slow transit constipation. this is likely also contributing to symptoms of gerd. even so, as her imaging was normal and symptoms persist despite treatment we will pursue further workup with both endoscopy and colonoscopy. she will utilize a miralax prep. i have asked her to restart lactulose twice daily and begin pantoprazole 40 mg daily, 30 minutes prior to breakfast. further recommendations to be made pending endoscopic results. we did review risk versus benefits of procedures especially given her age. she would like to pursue scopes at this time. an extensive discussion took place with the patient in regards to the risks and benefits of this procedure. i had spent some time discussing the risks which include anesthesia complications, risk of infection, bleeding, the risk of colonic perforation, and missed lesions with the patient. in addition, i have explained the importance of an adequate bowel preparation for evaluation purposes. the patient is aware that they cannot go back to work the day of the procedure and cannot drive the day of the procedure and will need someone to drive them home. they are encouraged to reach out to us if they have any additional questions.

entity: increased lymphoplasmacytic cells in the lamina propria
subject: PATIENT
heading: CC__page_no__16
text: received in formalin are 4 fragments of tan, soft tissue measuring 0.3 x 0.3 x 0.1 cm to 0.2 x 0.1 x 0.1 cm and submitted in toto in 1 cassette. | roscopic description | note | gastric mucosa shows increased lymphoplasmacytic cells in the lamina propria. neutrophilic activity is identified. no intestinal metaplasia is seen. no dysplasia or malignancy is seen. positive for helicobacter pylori (scattered organisms). buffered differential wright-giemsa stain is performed to enhance the detection of helicobacter pylori. the positive staining confirms the presence of helicobacter pylori as noted above. there is parietal cell hyperplasia with enlargement and dilatation of the lumens of some oxyntic glands. an additional minute fragment of unremarkable small bowel mucosa is also seen compatible with a carryover. | agnosis | note | transitional and oxyntic mucosa with active moderate chronic gastritis. positive for helicobacter pylori.
